original_abstract:
Unraveling the atomic configuration of a catalyst under operational conditions is essential to identifying the fundamental drivers of its performance. Because a catalyst’s structure rarely remains static when transitioning from synthesis to an active reaction environment, in situ characterization is the gold standard for such studies. In this work, we employ in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to investigate $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ hollow nanoframes, a class of materials recognized as world-class electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). 

To probe the surface properties of these nanoframes, we utilized electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition ($\text{H}_{\text{upd}}$) and carbon monoxide electrooxidation, revealing that structural variations in the nanoframes lead to distinct binding strengths with adsorbate molecules. It is established that the formation of a "Pt-skin" on Pt-Ni alloys significantly boosts ORR activity by optimizing (weakening) these surface binding energies. Our comparative ex situ and in situ XAS analysis demonstrates that nanoframes with stronger adsorbate binding possess a rougher Pt surface, a result of insufficient Pt segregation and subsequent Ni dissolution. In contrast, nanoframes exhibiting exceptionally high ORR activity show a more pronounced segregation of Pt over Ni-rich subsurface layers, facilitating the formation of a high-quality Pt-skin. This study highlights that the superior catalytic performance of $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ nanoframes is directly contingent upon the successful atomic-level engineering of the Pt-skin surface structure.
humanized_abstract:
Understanding the structure of a catalyst under working conditions is crucial for elucidating the factors that determine its performance. The structure of a catalyst is not invariant in going from the synthesis state to the working catalytic state and therefore in situ studies are generally the most meaningful. Here, in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) is employed to elucidate the structure of $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ hollow nanoframes while in operation as a world-class catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR).

To investigate the surface properties of the nanoframes, the characteristics of the electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition (Hupd) and carbon monoxide (CO) electrooxidation are compared, and the influence of the structure of nanoframes on the adsorbate binding strength is studied. The influence of the Pt-skin on the oxidation reaction rate (ORR) and the surface binding energy is investigated. Through the comparative analysis of the ex situ and in situ XAS of the nanoframes, the surface characteristics of the nanoframes with strong and weak adsorbate binding are obtained, and the relationship between the ORR activity and the surface characteristics is revealed. It is found that the nanoframes with strong adsorbate binding have a rougher Pt surface, and the insufficient Pt segregation and Ni dissolution lead to a lower ORR activity. Conversely, the nanoframes with the highest ORR activity show the highest degree of Pt segregation to the Ni-rich subsurface layers, indicating the formation of a high-quality Pt-skin. The superior performance of the $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ nanoframes is determined to depend on the precise control of the surface structure of the Pt-skin.

document.output:

Through the comparative analysis of the ex situ and in situ XAS of the nanoframes, the surface characteristics of the nanoframes with strong and weak adsorbate binding are obtained, and the relationship between the ORR activity and the surface characteristics is revealed. document.input:
Unraveling the atomic configuration of a catalyst under operational conditions is essential to identifying the fundamental drivers of its performance. Because a catalyst’s structure rarely remains static when transitioning from synthesis to an active reaction environment, in situ characterization is the gold standard for such studies.
